perturbation.original_text: During this period the receiving party shall not disclose the received “Confidential Information” to any third party, and sha ll not use the received “Confidential Information” for any purpose other than the afo resaid purposes.
perturbation.changed_text: During this period the receiving party shall endeavor not to disclose the received “Confidential Information” to any third party, and may use the received “Confidential Information” for purposes it deems generally consistent with the aforesaid purposes.
perturbation.explanation: The phrase "endeavor not to disclose" weakens the obligation to a mere attempt, and "generally consistent" introduces ambiguity as to what constitutes permissible use, contradicting the initial intention of strict confidentiality.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Florida Statutes § 812.081 - Theft of Trade Secrets
perturbation.law_explanation: Florida Statute 812.081 defines theft of trade secrets, which involves unauthorized use or disclosure. Changing 'shall not disclose' to 'endeavor not to disclose' provides a loophole, possibly conflicting with the legal protections against trade secret misappropriation under Florida law. Similarly, changing the usage clause opens the door to uses other than those agreed upon.

F.S. 812.081 812.081 Trade secrets; theft, embezzlement; unlawful copying; definitions; penalty. — (1) As used in this section: (a) “Article” means any object, device, machine, material, substance, or composition of matter, or any mixture or copy thereof, whether in whole or in part, including any complete or partial writing, record, recording, drawing, sample, specimen, prototype model, photograph, microorganism, blueprint, map, or copy thereof. (b) “Representing” means completely or partially describing, depicting, embodying, containing, constituting, reflecting, or recording. (c) “Trade secret” means the whole or any portion or phase of any formula, pattern, device, combination of devices, or compilation of information which is for use, or is used, in the operation of a business and which provides the business an advantage, or an opportunity to obtain an advantage, over those who do not know or use it. “Trade secret” includes any scientific, technical, or commercial information, including any design, process, procedure, list of suppliers, list of customers, business code, or improvement thereof. Irrespective of novelty, invention, patentability, the state of the prior art, and the level of skill in the business, art, or field to which the subject matter pertains, a trade secret is considered to be: 1. Secret; 2. Of value; 3. For use or in use by the business; and 4. Of advantage to the business, or providing an opportunity to obtain an advantage, over those who do not know or use it when the owner thereof takes measures to prevent it from becoming available to persons other than those selected by the owner to have access thereto for limited purposes. (d) “Copy” means any facsimile, replica, photograph, or other reproduction in whole or in part of an article and any note, drawing, or sketch made of or from an article or part or portion thereof. (2) Any person who, with intent to deprive or withhold from the owner thereof the control of a trade secret, or with an intent to app
perturbation.justification: The modified contract introduces ambiguities that undermine the original intent of strict confidentiality, potentially conflicting with trade secret protections under Florida Statute 812.081. The original text used mandatory language ('shall not disclose'), whereas the changed text uses weaker, discretionary language ('endeavor not to disclose'). Furthermore, the changed text introduces ambiguity regarding permitted uses of confidential information ('generally consistent'). According to Scraped Law Snippet 1, Florida Statute 812.081 defines trade secrets and addresses their theft or unauthorized use. The changes weaken the protection of trade secrets by making disclosure less strictly prohibited and usage more open to interpretation, which could conflict with the statute's aim to prevent the deprivation of control over trade secrets.

perturbation.original_text: The receiving party further agrees to use the same degree of care, but no less than a reasonable degree of care, with any “Confidential Information” as it would with its own confidential information, and to limit dissemination of the “Con fidential Information” to such of its employees who have a need to know of the “Confidential Information.”
perturbation.changed_text: The receiving party agrees to use a degree of care it considers adequate with any “Confidential Information” and to disseminate the “Confidential Information” to employees it believes may benefit from knowing it.
perturbation.explanation: Replacing "no less than a reasonable degree of care" with "a degree of care it considers adequate" introduces subjectivity. Similarly, changing "need to know" to "may benefit from knowing it" broadens the scope of dissemination, increasing the risk of disclosure.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Florida Statutes § 812.081 - Theft of Trade Secrets
perturbation.law_explanation: Florida Statute 812.081 aims to prevent unauthorized use and disclosure of trade secrets. By allowing the receiving party to determine what is 'adequate' care and who 'may benefit,' the agreement provides weaker protection than what is afforded by law regarding what constitutes a reasonable level of protection for confidential information, potentially leading to trade secret misappropriation.

Secret; 2. Of value; 3. For use or in use by the business; and 4. Of advantage to the business, or providing an opportunity to obtain an advantage, over those who do not know or use it when the owner thereof takes measures to prevent it from becoming available to persons other than those selected by the owner to have access thereto for limited purposes. (d) “Copy” means any facsimile, replica, photograph, or other reproduction in whole or in part of an article and any note, drawing, or sketch made of or from an article or part or portion thereof. (2) Any person who, with intent to deprive or withhold from the owner thereof the control of a trade secret, or with an intent to app
perturbation.justification: The modified text weakens the protection of trade secrets, potentially contradicting Florida Statute 812.081. The original text mandates "no less than a reasonable degree of care," establishing a clear, objective standard. The changed text replaces this with "a degree of care it considers adequate," introducing a subjective standard determined by the receiving party. Similarly, the original "need to know" limitation on dissemination is replaced with "may benefit from knowing it," broadening the scope and increasing the risk of unauthorized disclosure. According to the law snippet (F.S. 812.081 (2)), actions that show 'intent to deprive or withhold from the owner thereof the control of a trade secret' can be penalized. By allowing the receiving party to decide what is adequate and who may benefit, the modified agreement offers significantly less protection, increasing the risk of misappropriation.

perturbation.original_text: At the instruction of disclosing party, the receiving party agrees to either r eturn or dispose of all information received under this agreement, discontinue any use of the “Confidential Information”, and provide written confirmation thereof.
perturbation.changed_text: At the request of the disclosing party, the receiving party will consider returning or disposing of information received and may discontinue use of the “Confidential Information” as reasonably practical.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'instruction' to 'request' weakens the command. Replacing 'agrees to return or dispose' with 'will consider returning or disposing' introduces discretion. Removing the mandatory 'discontinue any use' and adding 'as reasonably practical' further weakens the obligation.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Florida Statutes § 812.081 - Theft of Trade Secrets
perturbation.law_explanation: Florida Statute 812.081 protects against the ongoing use of stolen trade secrets. If the receiving party only 'considers' returning the information and continues to use the confidential information because it is 'not reasonably practical' to discontinue use, they could be in violation of this Statute. The original language would have held them to a stricter standard, while the changes in this text introduce doubt.

F.S. 812.081 812.081 Theft of or trafficking in trade secrets; definitions; penalties; providing to foreign entities; restitution. — (1) As used in this section, the term: (a) “Endeavor” means to attempt or to try. (b) “Foreign agent” means any officer, employee, proxy, servant, delegate, or representative of a foreign government. (c) “Foreign instrumentality” means any agency, bureau, ministry, component, institution, association, or any legal, commercial, or business organization, corporation, firm, or entity that is substantially owned, controlled, sponsored, commanded, managed, or dominated by a foreign government. (d) “Obtain or use” has the same meaning as provided in s. 812.012 (3). (e) “Person” means a natural person, corporation, business trust, estate, trust, partnership, association, joint venture, government, governmental subdivision or agency, or any other legal or commercial entity. (f) “Trade secret” means the whole or any portion or phase of any formula, pattern, device, combination of devices, or compilation of information which is for use, or is used, in the operation of a business and which provides the business an advantage, or an opportunity to obtain an advantage, over those who do not know or use it. The term includes any scientific, technical, or commercial information, including financial information, and includes any design, process, procedure, list of suppliers, list of customers, business code, or improvement thereof, whether tangible or intangible, and regardless of whether or how it is stored, compiled, or memorialized physically, electronically, graphically, photographically, or in writing. Irrespective of novelty, invention, patentability, the state of the prior art, and the level of skill in the business, art, or field to which the subject matter pertains, a trade secret is considered to be: 1. Secret; 2. Of value; 3. For use or in use by the business; and 4. Of advantage to the business, or providing an opportunity to obtain an advantage, over
perturbation.justification: The changed text weakens the obligation to protect trade secrets, potentially violating Florida Statute 812.081. The original text mandates the return or disposal of confidential information and discontinuance of its use, whereas the changed text only requires the receiving party to 'consider' returning or disposing of the information and discontinue use only as 'reasonably practical.' According to the scraped law snippet, F.S. 812.081 concerns '[t]heft of or trafficking in trade secrets,' and defines 'trade secret' broadly. By introducing discretion and potentially allowing continued use of trade secrets, the changed text contradicts the intent of the statute to protect against unauthorized use and potential theft of trade secrets. The change introduces an ambiguity that would allow continued use under certain circumstances, creating a legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement sets forth the entire understanding between the parties with respect to the subject matter herein, and merges and supersedes all prior written agreements, discussions and understandings, expressed or implied, concerning such matters.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement represents a good faith effort to capture the primary understandings between the parties with respect to the core subject matter herein, and is intended to generally supersede prior agreements where explicitly inconsistent.
perturbation.explanation: Changing "entire understanding" to "good faith effort to capture the primary understandings" suggests the agreement may not be comprehensive. Adding "core" before "subject matter" limits the scope. Replacing "merges and supersedes all prior" with "generally supersede prior agreements where explicitly inconsistent" weakens the integration clause.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Florida Statutes § 672.202 - Final Written Expression: Parol or Extrinsic Evidence
perturbation.law_explanation: This change weakens the integration clause of the contract. Florida Statutes § 672.202 discusses parol evidence rule, which generally prevents the introduction of prior agreements to contradict a final written agreement. By saying the agreement only represents a 'good faith effort' and 'generally supersede prior agreements,' there is room to argue that prior agreements and discussions still apply, contradicting the purpose of an integration clause.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: § 2-202. Final Written Expression: Parol or Extrinsic Evidence. | Uniform Commercial Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you § 2-202. Final Written Expression: Parol or Extrinsic Evidence. Terms with respect to which the confirmatory memoranda of the parties agree or which are otherwise set forth in a writing intended by the parties as a final expression of their agreement with respect to such terms as are included therein may not be contradicted by evidence of any prior agreement or of a contemporaneous oral agreement but may be explained or supplemented (a) by course of dealing or usage of trade (Section 1-205 ) or by course of performance (Section 2-208 ); and (b) by evidence of consistent additional terms unless the court finds the writing to have been intended also as a complete and exclusive statement of the terms of the agreement . Uniform Commercial Code Toolbox About Uniform Laws State Uniform Commercial Codes
perturbation.justification: The changed text weakens the original integration clause, creating ambiguity regarding the agreement's comprehensiveness. The original text states the agreement sets forth the 'entire understanding,' while the modified text describes it as a 'good faith effort to capture the primary understandings,' suggesting incompleteness. Further, the change from 'merges and supersedes all prior' to 'generally supersede prior agreements where explicitly inconsistent' weakens the clause. Scraped Law Snippet 1, referencing UCC § 2-202, discusses the parol evidence rule, which aims to prevent prior agreements from contradicting a final written agreement. The weakened integration clause in the changed text allows for potential arguments that prior agreements still apply, directly contradicting the purpose of an integration clause and potentially undermining the parol evidence rule as described in the law snippet.
